title: SEGMENT 6 — T-DXd — Enhertu and the second-generation revolution

lines.text: SEGMENT 6 — T-DXd — Enhertu and the second-generation revolution
In December 2019, the FDA approved trastuzumab deruxtecan, marketed as Enhertu, abbreviated T-DXd.
The T is the same trastuzumab antibody as in T-DM1.
The DXd is the payload — a derivative of exatecan, which is a topoisomerase I inhibitor.
The dash in between is a different linker.
Same target, same antibody, different warhead, different rope.
And it turns out, when you change those last two things, you get a fundamentally different drug — one that has rewritten the textbook on breast cancer in five years.
Let me give you the headline numbers, because they're staggering.
In HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer, T-DXd was compared head-to-head against T-DM1 in a trial called DESTINY-Breast03.
The progression-free survival on T-DXd was about twenty-eight months.
On T-DM1, it was about seven months.
Four times longer.
That kind of effect size is rare in oncology.
We usually celebrate two-month survival improvements.
But the truly disruptive result came from a trial called DESTINY-Breast04, published in 2022.
That trial enrolled patients with HER2-low breast cancer — meaning their tumors had some HER2 staining, but not enough to qualify as HER2-positive.
This is a patient population that was previously thought to be outside the reach of HER2-targeted therapy entirely.
T-DM1 had been tried in HER2-low disease and failed.
The consensus was that you needed HER2-positive expression for HER2-directed treatment to work.
Period.
T-DXd ignored this consensus.
In DESTINY-Breast04, T-DXd extended progression-free survival from about five months to about ten months in HER2-low metastatic breast cancer.
The hazard ratio for death was around 0.64 — a thirty-six percent reduction in mortality.
Overnight, the field had a new patient population to treat.
Sixty percent of breast cancers, give or take, have some HER2 expression.
T-DXd extended the addressable population from twenty percent of breast cancers to potentially eighty percent.
So what changed?
How did a drug that looks superficially similar to T-DM1 produce such dramatically different clinical results?
The answer is mostly in two design choices that we'll explore in much more depth in the next few segments — but I'll give you the executive summary now.
Choice one: the payload.
Instead of DM1, T-DXd carries deruxtecan, abbreviated DXd.
DXd is a topoisomerase I inhibitor — a class of drug that includes irinotecan and topotecan, which you may have heard of as standard chemotherapy agents.
Topo I inhibitors stabilize a transient DNA-protein complex that forms during DNA replication, generating DNA damage that triggers apoptosis.
DXd is the engineered, ADC-optimized version of exatecan, an experimental topo I inhibitor that was abandoned as a standalone drug because it was too toxic.
So once again — we're using a payload that's too toxic to give as a free drug, delivered selectively by an antibody.
Choice two: the linker.

T-DXd uses a cleavable tetrapeptide linker — specifically a glycine-glycine-phenylalanine-glycine peptide that's cut by lysosomal enzymes, similar in concept to the valine-citrulline linker used in Adcetris but with different specifics.
So unlike T-DM1's non-cleavable design, T-DXd's linker is cut inside the cell, releasing free DXd — not a charged DXd-amino-acid conjugate, but the actual small molecule.
That second choice is where the magic happens, and it's worth dwelling on for a moment.
The free DXd molecule, once released inside a target cell, is small and uncharged enough that it can diffuse across cell membranes.
It doesn't stay trapped inside the cell that internalized the ADC.
It leaks out — and leaks into neighboring cells.
Including, crucially, neighboring cells that don't express HER2.
And in those neighboring cells, the DXd does its job — it inhibits topoisomerase I, generates DNA damage, kills the cell.
This is the bystander effect, and it is so important that I'm going to dedicate the entire next segment to it.
But you can already see the shape of the implication.
With T-DM1, only HER2-positive cells die.
With T-DXd, HER2-positive cells die and so do the neighboring cells, regardless of their HER2 expression.
In a tumor where HER2 expression is heterogeneous — high in some areas, low in others — T-DXd can reach the low-HER2 cells through bystander killing.
T-DM1 cannot.
That, in a sentence, is why T-DXd works in HER2-low disease where T-DM1 doesn't.
The other design choice worth mentioning is the drug-antibody ratio.
T-DM1 has an average DAR of about 3.5 — meaning each antibody carries, on average, about three and a half DM1 molecules.
T-DXd has a DAR of 8 — each antibody carries eight DXd molecules.
More than double the warheads per missile.
And this was a deliberate choice by the Daiichi Sankyo team that designed T-DXd, but it was also risky.
Historically, high-DAR ADCs had been shown to have worse pharmacokinetic properties — they got cleared from circulation faster, they aggregated in solution, they were more toxic.
The conventional wisdom was that DAR should be kept in the 2 to 4 range.
T-DXd doubled that, and made it work, by engineering the linker and payload to be more hydrophilic — more water-loving — so the high-DAR molecule still behaved well in plasma.
We'll do a whole segment on DAR.
But for now: T-DXd carries twice the payload of T-DM1, and it carries a payload that, once released, can leak to kill neighboring cells.
Different drug, different physics, different clinical reality.
By 2024, T-DXd was generating over five billion dollars in annual revenue.
It's been approved in HER2-positive breast cancer, HER2-low breast cancer, HER2-mutant lung cancer, HER2-positive gastric cancer, and several other indications.
It is one of the most successful drug launches in oncology history.

And it is, in retrospect, the drug that proved second-generation ADC design — with cleavable linkers, bystander-capable payloads, and high DAR — could outperform first-generation ADCs by a margin that surprised even the developers.
Daiichi Sankyo, the Japanese company that developed T-DXd in partnership with AstraZeneca, became overnight one of the most valuable ADC companies in the world.
We'll come back to their pipeline.
But first, let's understand the bystander effect — because it's the central pharmacological superpower that makes T-DXd what it is.
